title: Βάτραχοι αἰτοῦντες βασιλέα
titleEn: The Frogs Who Asked for a King
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 66 (= Perry 44). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« », ⟨ ⟩ for editorial supplements). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Βάτραχοι λυπούμενοι ἐπὶ τῇ ἑαυτῶν ἀναρχίᾳ πρέσβεις ἔπεμψαν πρὸς τὸν Δία, δεόμενοι βασιλέα αὐτοῖς παρασχεῖν.
sentences.tr: Batrachoi lypoumenoi epi tēi heautōn anarchiāi presbeis epempsan pros ton Dia, deomenoi basilea autois paraschein.
sentences.l: Frogs, grieving over the of-themselves anarchy, ambassadors sent to Zeus, begging a king to-them to-furnish.
sentences.i: The frogs, unhappy with their own lawless state, sent ambassadors to Zeus begging him to provide them a king.
sentences.n: ἀναρχία is literally 'rulerlessness' — our 'anarchy' straight from the Greek. Δία is the accusative of Ζεύς (the stem is Δι-, as in Διός, Διί). πρέσβεις, 'elders' → 'ambassadors' — the ordinary word for an official delegation.

sentences.t: Ὁ δὲ συνιδὼν τὴν εὐήθειαν αὐτῶν ξύλον εἰς τὴν λίμνην καθῆκε.
sentences.tr: Ho de synidōn tēn euētheian autōn xylon eis tēn limnēn kathēke.
sentences.l: And he, having-perceived the simple-mindedness of-them, a log into the pond let-down.
sentences.i: Zeus, seeing how simple they were, dropped a log into their pond.
sentences.n: εὐήθεια — literally 'good-naturedness', but in Greek it slid into 'naïveté, silliness'; the word itself is a small lesson in irony.

sentences.t: Καὶ οἱ βάτραχοι, τὸ μὲν πρῶτον καταπλαγέντες τὸν ψόφον, εἰς τὰ βάθη τῆς λίμνης ἐνέδυσαν.
sentences.tr: Kai hoi batrachoi, to men prōton kataplagentes ton psophon, eis ta bathē tēs limnēs enedysan.
sentences.l: And the frogs, at the first terrified-by the splash, into the depths of-the pond dove.
sentences.i: At first the frogs were terrified by the splash and dove to the bottom of the pond.

sentences.t: Ὕστερον δέ, ὡς ἀκίνητον ἦν τὸ ξύλον, ἀναδύντες εἰς τοσοῦτον καταφρονήσεως ἦλθον ὡς ἐπιβαίνοντες αὐτῷ ἐπικαθέζεσθαι.
sentences.tr: Hysteron de, hōs akinēton ēn to xylon, anadyntes eis tosouton kataphronēseōs ēlthon hōs epibainontes autōi epikathezesthai.
sentences.l: But later, when motionless was the log, having-come-up they into so-much of-contempt came as, climbing-on it, to-sit-upon-it.
sentences.i: But later, since the log never moved, they surfaced — and came to despise it so thoroughly that they climbed up and sat on it.
sentences.n: εἰς τοσοῦτον καταφρονήσεως ἦλθον ὡς + infinitive: 'they came to such a pitch of contempt as to…' — a favorite Greek intensifier; the genitive (καταφρονήσεως) names what they came to a pitch of.

sentences.t: Ἀναξιοπαθοῦντες δὲ τοιοῦτον ἔχειν βασιλέα, ἧκον ἐκ δευτέρου πρὸς τὸν Δία καὶ τοῦτον παρεκάλουν ἀλλάξαι αὐτοῖς τὸν ἄρχοντα·
sentences.tr: Anaxiopathountes de toiouton echein basilea, hēkon ek deuterou pros ton Dia kai touton parekaloun allaxai autois ton archonta:
sentences.l: And thinking-it-beneath-them such-a-one to-have as-king, they-came a second-time to Zeus and him they-entreated to-change for-them the ruler:
sentences.i: Feeling it beneath them to have such a king, they went to Zeus a second time and begged him to change rulers for them,

sentences.t: τὸν γὰρ πρῶτον λίαν εἶναι νωχελῆ.
sentences.tr: ton gar prōton lian einai nōchelē.
sentences.l: for the first to-be excessively sluggish.
sentences.i: for the first one, they said, was far too sluggish.
sentences.n: Reported speech in bare accusative + infinitive (τὸν πρῶτον … εἶναι), the γάρ carrying their 'because' — no 'they said' needed; the construction itself signals quotation.

sentences.t: Καὶ ὁ Ζεὺς ἀγανακτήσας καθ' αὑτῶν ὕδρον αὐτοῖς ἔπεμψεν, ὑφ' οὗ συλλαμβανόμενοι κατησθίοντο.
sentences.tr: Kai ho Zeus aganaktēsas kath' hautōn hydron autois epempsen, hyph' hou syllambanomenoi katēsthionto.
sentences.l: And Zeus, having-grown-indignant against them, a water-snake to-them sent, by whom being-caught they-were-devoured.
sentences.i: And Zeus, exasperated with them, sent them a water-snake — which caught them and ate them, one after another.
sentences.n: The imperfect κατησθίοντο is grim: not 'were eaten' once, but 'kept being eaten' — the new king's reign as an ongoing process.

sentences.t: Ὁ λόγος δηλοῖ ὅτι ἄμεινόν ἐστι νωθεῖς καὶ μὴ πονηροὺς ἔχειν ἄρχοντας ἢ ταρακτικοὺς καὶ κακούργους.
sentences.tr: Ho logos dēloi hoti ameinon esti nōtheis kai mē ponērous echein archontas ē taraktikous kai kakourgous.
sentences.l: The story shows that better it-is sluggish and not wicked to-have rulers than turbulent and evil-doing ones.
sentences.i: The story shows that it is better to have rulers who are lazy but harmless than energetic troublemakers.
